sentences.text: Yes, I went to--
Yeah, married, and, and, as I mentioned earlier, lived on the campus of UCLA, and in a curriculum for mechanical engineering at UCLA.
So there was a lot of math and, and engineering subjects and physics and chemistry, et cetera.
I graduated in 1950, June of '50, and went to work immediately for a-- an appliance company in South Los Angeles.
And had, had actually moved back to my mother's residence after we graduated-- mother's former residence.
So we lived there.
And I drove into Los Angeles at this appliance company, but I had-- I had made the application to a-- to a place called AeroJet General in Azusa, California, east of Los Angeles.
I got tired of the commute to downtown Los Angeles, or South Los Angeles, and I accepted a position at Lockheed in Burbank.
And I stayed there only for a couple of months, because I got an offer from Aerojet General, which was really where I wanted to go in the first place.
So I, I started my career in rocket propulsion at Aerojet General in Azusa, California.
I was assigned initially as a test engineer to test rockets-- rocket engines.
We tested several pretty advanced kinds of rockets that we were trying to develop using a, a mono-propellant, for instance.
The Navy-- we were involved-- Aerojet was involved in assist takeoff units, solid rocket units.

And we were getting, in those days, in 1950 when I went to work-- '51, when I went to work for Aerojet-- they were trying to develop a-- an assist takeoff unit for the Navy, which was liquid rather than solid propellant.
And they didn't like to have to buy propellants, like an oxidizer and a fuel on board a ship, particularly on a carrier.
So we were trying to develop a mono-propellant.
And we were using nitromethane.
Nitromethane, as you may know, is used in race cars-- [LAUGHS] high-powered race cars.
And, so we were trying to develop this nitromethane assist takeoff unit engine, and we had all kinds of fun with that.
One time, we had an explosion.
And it tore the engine off the test stand and threw it for half a mile.
[LAUGHS]
So I worked at, at Aerojet as a test engineer for a long time, and then got into working on other kinds of propulsion types of programs.
Spent, let's see, from 1951 till 1964 [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] working on various kinds of propulsion devices.
One for an ICBM, which was the Titan ICBM, intercontinental ballistic missile.
Aerojet was responsible for the engines on that, and I, I designed and, and had built elements of those engines, gas generators, et cetera.
And then I was offered a, a position at Jet Propulsion Laboratory in Pasadena.
And that's where my experience in unmanned spacecraft came to fore.

I was-- after I accepted the offer and went to work at JPL, I was assigned to, to monitor and direct the contract with an organization in New Jersey called Thiokol, who was building the rocket engines for the Surveyor spacecraft, which was a moon lander.
It was the preamble to the Apollo program.
We sent up-- actually built, built seven spacecraft.
Actually, JPL didn't build them, but we were the contractors and had Hughes Aircraft in El Segundo build them.
We sent seven up, and five were successful and landed on the moon and found out a lot about the moon.

And I also gave a talk about that [LAUGHS] to various organizations, one of which is that there was a, a, a, big concern that the moon-- and particularly espoused by one scientist-- that the moon had a, a layer of 15 to 20 feet of fine dust.
And this man said, you, you can't send people to the moon because they'll get swallowed up by this dust layer.
You can't do that.
And so we sent the surveyors up to find out if that was the case.
And it wasn't.
The moon is-- insofar as the solidity of the surface, is just like Earth, except it doesn't have any water.
And so we, we sent the five that were successful, showed that it was OK to send men there.
So that kind of was the precursor to the Apollo program.
And we know what happened on the Apollo program.
We sent men to the moon, and it was highly successful.
And that really was the start of my career in, in rocket propulsion.
And I'm in quotes told I'm a 'rocket scientist.' [LAUGHS] And a lot of people get a kick out a calling me a rocket scientist, but I-- I guess I am in, in some way.
After that program, I, I was assigned to what was called Mariner, the Mariner Mars 71.
And we sent a spacecraft to orbit Mars.
It was launched aboard an Atlas-Centaur launch vehicle, one of which failed and plunked our spacecraft into the ocean.
[LAUGHS] But the other one got there OK and orbited Mars and, and photographed about 80% of the surface of Mars.
And, and that told us a lot about one of our sister planets.
Following that, I, I was assigned to a, a program called Viking, which was a Mars lander.
And we launched that and orbited Mars and set the lander down on the surface of Mars to look for life.
And we didn't find any.
Now, more recently, there was a big hoopla by NASA, National Aeronautics and Space Administration, that they have found a rock in the Antarctic.
And they're analyzing that rock.
And, and they think that if they find life in that rock, that there's life on Mars.
We didn't find any with Viking.
Of course, we were in one spot with the lander.
And we dug up the Martian soil and didn't find any, any life in that episode.

But there is a big plan, as I understand, to send unmanned rovers to Mars and rove around the surface and take samples and see if, indeed, there could have been-- could've been life on Mars some eons ago.
Following that, I worked on a-- on a program called Voyager, which was a fly-by of Saturn and Jupiter and Uranus, Uranus and Neptune.
And that was in 1977.
And those two spacecraft are way out beyond the solar system by now.
And after that program, I left the employ of JPL and went to-- because I had met Marge in 1960.
She had-- she was my secretary at the time at Aerojet.
And in 1977, we were married.
And I was working at JPL.
And she had moved a company that she had been founder of in Orange County, moved it to Monterey.
And so here's, here's my-- well, it's actually before we were married.
She moved the company up here in 1973.
And we had been dating 'cause I had-- my wife-- former wife had been-- and I had divorced.
And so here I am, working in Pasadena, and she's up here now in Monterey.
And I'm commuting every weekend, living in an apartment down there.
And that was getting kind of dumb.
So we decided that I was going to leave JPL.
And so I did in 1978 and went to work for Lockheed in Sunnyvale.
That was another commute.
And that got, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] got poor after a while.
This, this commute was getting to me.
I was a basket case by Friday.
So I told Lockheed I was gonna leave.
And they said, but you can't.
We're working on an important program.
It was called HOE, Homing Overlay Experiment, which was an interceptor that was supposed to knock down a reentry vehicle from a Russian ICBM up above the atmosphere.
And so I worked on that program on the propulsion system again.
And, but I said, I, I, I can't do this anymore.
So they offered me a consulting contract, which allowed me to stay up there in a hotel.
And, and they paid for my food and transportation.
And I'd come home a couple times a week versus every day.
And I worked there as a-- as a consultant for two years.
And then, then I started branching out to other pursuits in the-- in the consulting business.
And I went to-- got a consulting contract from Ford Aerospace in Palo Alto.
Same situation there, where I worked on the propulsion system for satellites-- communications satellites.
One for NOAA, National Oceanographic and Atmospheric Association, which was one of the weather satellites that we see on TV now.
You see the, the cloud formations and the weather patterns.
And, and this satellite up 22,000 miles sees that.
And that's what you see on the weather, weather broadcasts on the news programs.
So I worked on that one.
And I also worked on a communication satellite for Japan, which is now, now doing the same sort of thing over Japan.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Worked there until that contract ran out.
And let's see, where did I go?
Oh, I went into-- to other, other contracting efforts at Rocketdyne and Canoga Park and Hughes and El Segundo.
And did a lot of-- my forte being rocket propulsion, they needed consulting in those areas.
So I consulted, essentially, until I retired.
We-- Marge and I formed a, a consulting firm called Marunn, Marge's first name-- first of hers-- her name and the last of my name.
Called it Marunn Consultants, Inc.
[LAUGHS] So we worked out of that until I retired in '89.
And she continued to do a fair amount of consulting in her field until, oh gosh, she consulted for [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] another four years, I guess.
I was really-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] during the war and after the war, I really-- I don't say I didn't have any faith, but I was not heavily involved in, in the religious aspects of life.
We went to church, Presbyterian, when I was married to, to Sally in-- lived in Glendora.
We had-- we had moved from North Hollywood to Baldwin Park.
And then I had a house built in Glendora, and I lived there while I worked at Aerojet General.
And really, I wasn't-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I had a lot of faith in God.
I felt that there was a supreme being, and I had faith.
But I wasn't a, a very energetic churchgoer.
I was reluctant.
No, not anymore.
[LAUGHS] I-- of course, being-- Marge was very much a, a person involved in her church in Massachusetts.
And when we were married, I wasn't really enthusiastic about church.
But she was, and she missed it a lot.
And so we finally kind of got our minds together.
And since we'd been married in the Episcopal Church in Carmel, we, we, we tried that church for a while, and that didn't work too well.
So we started church hunting.
And we ended up in the First Methodist Church in Pacific Grove.
And we were enthralled with the minister, who was a black man named Tom Kimball, just a, a extremely loving, very talented, energetic guy.
And so we, we fell in love with that church right away.
And, and we started going every Sunday, and, and became involved, and joined the church, became involved in committees.
And I'm presently on the Board of Trustees and on the Nominating Committee, and I do things for the church.
I think I mentioned earlier to you when I was talking about refurbishing the beautiful sanctuary doors of the church.
So I, I have a great deal of faith and interest in, in that church and all that goes with it.
Yeah.
As a matter of fact, I'll backtrack a little bit.
When I was consulting at Lockheed, I had decided I wanted to, as an avocation, remodel houses.
My first job was for our attorney.
And he wanted me to refurbish-- remodel his Northstar Tahoe cabin.
It was more a house than a cabin.
The unfortunate part of the-- of that cabin was that it had a flat roof.
And in Northstar [LAUGHS] Tahoe, it snows a lot.
And that house leaked like a sieve.
So he asked me, could I put a, a pitched roof on it and do some other things, like put in a hot tub, and put in a new water heater, and a bunch of things?
And so that was my first real experience.
But I was talking about a different talent.
I understand that you have a very nice voice.
Oh, oh, I thought you were talking about my carpentry at the church.
[LAUGHTER] Well, I'm told I have.
As a matter of fact, I, I do sing with the chorus at Monterey Peninsula College.
I'll sing a little bit of Frank Sinatra.
And I've forgotten what the name of it is.
But anyway, I'll sing it.
[SINGING] It seems we stood and talked like this before.
We looked at each other in the same way then.
But I can't remember where or when.
The clothes you're wearing are the clothes you wore.
The smile you are smiling, you were smiling then.
But I can't remember where or when.
Some things that happened for a first time seem to be happening again.
And so it seems that we had met before, and laughed before, and loved before, but who knows where or when?
Thank you, beautiful.
Thank you.
It just came to me.
I, I like Frank Sinatra, like his 'That Old Black Magic' and other tunes he sings and has sung over the years.
And it just came to me that that was one of them that I, I like.
Yes.
I have three, and Marge has three.
So betwixt us, we have six.
We have one, two, three-- three grandchildren and two great-grandchildren.
Yes.
My, my children, the oldest is Shelley, Shelley Eileen.
Her name is Bochinno now.
She married-- or is married.
She lives in Virginia with her husband.
And, as a matter of fact, we went back to her marriage and--
No, she, she does not.
The middle--
The middle-- my middle daughter is Claudia, Claudia Melinda Hardwick.
She-- she's an entertainer.
And her husband's a stand-up comedian.
She performed at Disneyland at the Golden Horseshoe and-- along with him.
And she then sang at the Disneyland Hotel with a band for a long time.
And now she's out doing, doing those gigs on her own.
And he does, too.
He's always gone.
He's a very-- apparently, a very popular comedian 'cause he does-- he's doing quite well.
And then my son, son, James Christopher, he is a very, very talented musician.
As a matter of fact, he was a music minister for a very large church in Scottsdale, Arizona, and took charge of all of the music and performed at that church.
So he's-- he lives there with his, his wife, Robin, and their two children.
Julianne and Sarah.
Julianne-- let's see, Sarah is-- can't think of their middle names.
Julianne and Sarah Dunn.
Yes, I have, to a Rotary Club in Monterey, also to an organization I belong to called SIRs, Sons In Retirement.
I gave a talk to them about my Dachau experiences.
And I-- at first, I was a, a bit reluctant because of the trauma associated with it.
And, as a matter of fact, York School, which is a private school here in Monterey, talked to me about giving a talk to the students.
And I, I wondered whether that was appropriate.
I guess so, because they're high school age, some of them are, and those I would've talked to.
And that hasn't actually happened yet.
But I understand that they're interested in my giving that talk that I give about the Holocaust.
I have recently.
Not, not while I was married to Sally.
I, I never talked about it.
They need to know.
They're, they're certainly adults now, and they really need to know what went on.
So I've talked to them about it.
I haven't gone into the detail that we've-- that I've talked about here, but I've told them about what I experienced there and-- particularly to my son, Jimmy.
And I think they, they need to know the real truth.
Because they're hearing funny stuff from organizations and people that say that it never happened.
And, and I tell him that, well, it, it sure did.
And your dad went through it.
And so believe me, it happened.
So I do talk to them about it.
Oh, yes.
Yes, as a matter of fact, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was invited-- and maybe I should tell you the story about the 50th commemorative program in San Francisco.
How they got my name, I do not know.
But they did.
And they invited me and Marge to come to San Francisco to the El Shalom Temple to be a party to the 50th anniversary of the liberation of the death camps.
This was in May of '95.
And I was asked to come because I-- they learned that I was a liberator.
And I was very interested in going to hear others' viewpoints.
And, and they told me that there'd be survivors giving their testimony and other liberators.
And yes, I was interested.
And, and they-- we went there and were treated like absolute heroes.
You know, I talked about being a reluctant hero earlier [INAUDIBLE], but this was-- they treated us like we were some kinds of gods.
This one lady said-- of course, we were walking down to the dining room, I guess.
And she said to my wife, your husband was a liberator?
And she said, yes.
In what camp?
And she said, Dachau.
And she said she was a, a survivor from Belsen, I believe it was.
And, and she said, I kiss the ground of this country every day.
And without people like your husband, I wouldn't be able to do that.
And then we had this testimony by these several survivors, and then quite a story by one of them that was in Czechoslovakia.
She happened to be in, I guess, a satellite camp.
Because she was standing in the doorway of this factory and saw this vehicle coming down the hill.
And it had a white star on it.
And this person jumped out of the-- of the Jeep.
It was an American officer.
And he came running down to there.
And she was standing in the doorway.
And she said, 'Ich bin Juden.' And he said, 'So am I!' [LAUGHS] And so he was her liberator, and they were married a year later in Paris.
Yeah, what a story.
And she's-- in her testimony, she said, without you-- people like you-- I wouldn't be able to rock my grandchildren.
I wouldn't have been able to marry this fine man that I'm married to.
I wouldn't have-- I wouldn't have survived for another 50 years.
And stories like that, and I learned a great deal more about what went on in other areas of, of these death camps and from the liberators themselves.
I mentioned that the 45th Division personnel had actually preceded us to Dachau.
And I had sat next to one of the fellows that was in the 45th Division.
And he told me about this execution of the German guards.
They were captured and lined up against the wall and summarily executed.
And the leader of that company was court martialed but pardoned.
Because the reason that they did that, they were so angry at what had gone on, so angry that these people did not deserve to live.
The battalion commander was Colonel Walter Fellons.
I was a company commander.
And then his boss was Colonel Henry Luongo, L-U-O-N-G-O.
And the division commander was Major General Harry Collins.
